g.data-narration: Proving Languages Not to Be Regular - We study methods like Pumping Lemma and Myhill-Nerode to show languages cannot be recognized by finite automata.
g.text: Proving Languages Not to Be Regular

g.data-narration: Regular languages form the simplest class in the Chomsky hierarchy. Proving non-regularity helps understand finite automata limitations and when more powerful models are needed.
g.text: Why Prove Non-Regularity?
• Regular languages are recognized by Finite Automata (FA) with finite memory
• Many useful languages require infinite memory (like counting)
• Two main methods: Pumping Lemma and Myhill-Nerode Theorem

g.data-narration: The Pumping Lemma: If L is regular, there exists pumping length p where every string s in L with length at least p can be written as xyz with specific properties.
g.text: Pumping Lemma (Statement)
If L is regular, ∃ pumping length p such that ∀ strings s ∈ L with |s| ≥ p:
s can be divided as xyz where |y| > 0, |xy| ≤ p
and xyⁱz ∈ L for all i ≥ 0

g.data-narration: To prove non-regularity: Assume L is regular, choose string s with length at least p, then show all valid decompositions lead to contradiction.
g.text: How to Use Pumping Lemma
1. Assume L is regular (let p be the pumping length)
2. Choose a string s ∈ L where |s| ≥ p (choose wisely to cause contradiction)
3. Consider ALL decompositions xyz with |y| > 0 and |xy| ≤ p

g.data-narration: Continue proof: Show for any valid decomposition, pumping produces a string not in L, contradict lemma, conclude language is not regular.
g.text: Pumping Lemma Proof Steps (cont.)
4. Show ∀ decompositions, ∃ i ≥ 0 such that xyⁱz ∉ L
5. This contradicts Pumping Lemma → assumption false
6. Therefore, L is NOT regular

g.data-narration: Example: L = {aⁿbⁿ | n ≥ 0}. Choose s = aᵖbᵖ. Since |xy| ≤ p, both x and y contain only a's. Pumping y gives aᵖ⁺ᵏbᵖ which is not in L.
g.text: Example: L = {aⁿbⁿ | n ≥ 0}
1. Assume L is regular with pumping length p
2. Choose s = aᵖbᵖ ∈ L, |s| = 2p ≥ p
3. Since |xy| ≤ p, xy = aᵏ (only a's). Let y = aᵏ where k ≥ 1
4. For i = 2: xy²z = aᵖ⁺ᵏbᵖ. Since p+k ≠ p, not in L. Contradiction!

g.data-narration: Myhill-Nerode: Language L is regular iff equivalence relation R_L has finite equivalence classes. If infinite distinguishable strings exist, it proves non-regularity.
g.text: Myhill-Nerode Theorem
L is regular ⇔ number of equivalence classes in R_L is finite
Strings x, y distinguishable if ∃z such that exactly one of xz, yz ∈ L
For {aⁿbⁿ}: infinite distinguishable strings → L is NOT regular
